sectionName: Biological and Biochemical Foundations of Living Systems
topicArea: Renal Acid-Base Handling / Urea Cycle Disorders

questions.passage:
Renal compensation for metabolic acid-base disturbances involves coordinated changes in bicarbonate reabsorption, titratable acid excretion, and ammonium production. To examine these mechanisms under chronic acid loading, researchers administered 0.28 M ammonium chloride (NH4Cl) in drinking water to adult male Sprague-Dawley rats for 7 days. Control animals received untreated water. Arterial blood samples and 24-hour urine collections were obtained on days 0, 3, and 7.

Control rats maintained arterial pH at 7.38 ± 0.02, plasma [HCO3−] at 24.1 ± 1.2 mEq/L, and PaCO2 at 40 ± 2 mmHg throughout the study. In experimental animals, arterial pH fell to 7.22 ± 0.03 by day 3, with plasma [HCO3−] dropping to 14.6 ± 1.4 mEq/L and PaCO2 decreasing to 30 ± 3 mmHg. By day 7, arterial pH partially recovered to 7.30 ± 0.02, with plasma [HCO3−] rising to 17.8 ± 1.1 mEq/L.

Urine pH in acidotic rats fell from 6.2 at baseline to 5.0 by day 3 and remained near 5.1 through day 7. Urinary ammonium excretion increased from 42 ± 8 μmol/day at baseline to 185 ± 22 μmol/day by day 7, a 4.4-fold increase. Titratable acid excretion rose from 28 ± 5 μmol/day to 61 ± 9 μmol/day over the same period.

Immunoblot analysis of renal cortical homogenates demonstrated that mitochondrial glutaminase expression increased 3.2-fold by day 7, glutamate dehydrogenase (GDH) 2.1-fold, and cytosolic phosphoenolpyruvate carboxykinase (PEPCK) 2.8-fold. Na+/H+ exchanger 3 (NHE3) protein in proximal tubule brush border membranes rose 2.5-fold. In the outer medullary collecting duct, H+-ATPase abundance in type A intercalated cells increased 1.9-fold, while pendrin expression in type B intercalated cells fell to 40% of control values.

Figure 1 summarizes the time course of selected acid-base variables in the acid-loaded animals.
questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Experimental rats show early metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation, followed by increased renal ammonium excretion and partial pH recovery by day 7.
questions.stem: Based on the day 3 blood gas changes shown in Figure 1, the reduction in PaCO2 observed in the experimental group is best explained by:

choices.A: Increased renal ammonium production buffering arterial CO2
choices.B: Decreased alveolar ventilation secondary to acidemia-induced respiratory depression
choices.C: Increased alveolar ventilation driven by peripheral chemoreceptor stimulation in response to low arterial pH
choices.D: Enhanced carbonic anhydrase activity in erythrocytes converting CO2 to bicarbonate

explanations.A: Renal ammonium production is a renal compensatory mechanism and does not directly affect PaCO2. Arterial PaCO2 is determined by the balance between metabolic CO2 production and alveolar ventilation, not by renal ammonia handling.
explanations.B: Acidemia stimulates, rather than depresses, ventilation through activation of peripheral chemoreceptors in the carotid and aortic bodies. Decreased ventilation would raise PaCO2, which is the opposite of what was observed in the experimental group.
explanations.C: The decrease in PaCO2 from 40 to 30 mmHg reflects respiratory compensation for metabolic acidosis. Peripheral chemoreceptors in the carotid bodies detect the fall in arterial pH and increase ventilatory drive, producing deeper and more rapid breathing. This hyperventilation lowers PaCO2, partially offsetting the drop in pH. This is the expected acute compensatory response to a primary metabolic acidosis and is consistent with the day 3 blood gas data.
explanations.D: Carbonic anhydrase in erythrocytes catalyzes the interconversion of CO2 and bicarbonate continuously under normal conditions. This enzyme does not produce a net reduction in arterial PaCO2, which requires increased alveolar elimination of CO2 through hyperventilation.

questions.stem: The coordinated upregulation of glutaminase, GDH, and PEPCK in the renal cortex most directly facilitates:

choices.A: Gluconeogenesis from glutamine with concurrent generation of NH4+ and new bicarbonate
choices.B: Increased urea cycle flux to detoxify ammonia produced during amino acid catabolism
choices.C: Enhanced glycolysis in proximal tubule cells to support ATP-dependent acid secretion
choices.D: Beta-oxidation of fatty acids to meet the elevated energy demands of the acidotic kidney

explanations.A: During chronic metabolic acidosis the proximal tubule increases extraction of glutamine from peritubular blood. Glutaminase cleaves glutamine to glutamate and NH4+; GDH converts glutamate to alpha-ketoglutarate and a second NH4+. Alpha-ketoglutarate enters gluconeogenesis via PEPCK, ultimately producing glucose and generating new HCO3− that is returned to the blood. Each glutamine molecule thus yields two ammonium ions for urinary excretion and contributes to bicarbonate regeneration, directly linking all three upregulated enzymes to renal acid-base compensation.
explanations.B: The urea cycle operates in the liver, not the kidney. Renal ammoniagenesis generates NH4+ for excretion into the urine, not for conversion to urea. Upregulation of these renal enzymes supports ammonia production and gluconeogenesis, not hepatic urea synthesis.
explanations.C: Proximal tubule cells rely predominantly on oxidative metabolism of substrates such as fatty acids and glutamine, not glycolysis. PEPCK catalyzes a gluconeogenic reaction, not a glycolytic one, and glutaminase and GDH feed carbon skeletons into gluconeogenesis, not glycolysis.
explanations.D: While the kidney does oxidize fatty acids, the three enzymes cited in the passage—glutaminase, GDH, and PEPCK—are components of the glutamine-to-glucose pathway, not the beta-oxidation pathway. Their upregulation specifically reflects increased renal ammoniagenesis and gluconeogenesis.

questions.stem: The 2.5-fold increase in NHE3 protein on the proximal tubule apical membrane most directly contributes to acid-base compensation by:

choices.A: Transporting bicarbonate from peritubular blood into the tubular lumen to neutralize urinary acid
choices.B: Increasing potassium secretion into the filtrate in exchange for sodium reabsorption
choices.C: Facilitating paracellular chloride reabsorption to maintain electroneutrality during bicarbonate loss
choices.D: Secreting H+ into the filtrate, driving the reabsorption of filtered bicarbonate back into peritubular blood

explanations.A: NHE3 secretes H+ into the tubular lumen and does not transport bicarbonate into the lumen. Bicarbonate is reabsorbed, not secreted, in the proximal tubule during acid-base compensation. Adding bicarbonate to the lumen would waste base and worsen the acidosis.
explanations.B: NHE3 exchanges luminal Na+ for intracellular H+. It does not transport potassium. Potassium handling in the proximal tubule is primarily paracellular via solvent drag, and potassium secretion in the distal nephron is mediated by ROMK channels, not NHE3.
explanations.C: Although chloride reabsorption does occur in the proximal tubule, NHE3 is a Na+/H+ exchanger and does not directly mediate chloride transport. Paracellular chloride movement is driven by electrochemical gradients, not by NHE3 activity itself.
explanations.D: NHE3 on the apical membrane exchanges luminal Na+ for intracellular H+. The secreted H+ combines with filtered HCO3− to form H2CO3, which is rapidly dehydrated to CO2 and H2O by luminal carbonic anhydrase IV. CO2 diffuses into the cell, where carbonic anhydrase II regenerates HCO3−, which exits the basolateral membrane into peritubular capillary blood. Upregulation of NHE3 therefore increases the proximal tubule's capacity to reclaim filtered bicarbonate, a critical component of renal acid-base compensation.

questions.stem: The decrease in pendrin expression in type B intercalated cells during chronic acidosis is most consistent with:

choices.A: Increased paracellular bicarbonate reabsorption driven by a favorable lumen-to-blood concentration gradient
choices.B: Reduced apical bicarbonate secretion, conserving base that would otherwise be lost in the urine
choices.C: Enhanced ENaC-mediated sodium reabsorption in principal cells of the collecting duct
choices.D: Increased chloride secretion into the lumen, creating an osmotic gradient for water reabsorption

explanations.A: Bicarbonate reabsorption in the collecting duct is transcellular, not paracellular. It is achieved through H+ secretion by type A intercalated cells, not through passive paracellular movement. Pendrin downregulation affects transcellular bicarbonate secretion by type B cells specifically.
explanations.B: Pendrin is a Cl−/HCO3− exchanger on the apical membrane of type B intercalated cells that secretes bicarbonate into the tubular lumen and reabsorbs chloride. During metabolic acidosis, secreting bicarbonate into the urine would be counterproductive to restoring pH. Downregulating pendrin reduces this bicarbonate loss, helping the kidney conserve base. This change complements the increased H+ secretion by type A intercalated cells to maximize net acid excretion during chronic acid loading.
explanations.C: Pendrin is expressed on type B intercalated cells and is not functionally linked to ENaC on principal cells. ENaC activity is regulated primarily by aldosterone and is not directly affected by pendrin expression. These are distinct cell types with separate transport functions.
explanations.D: Pendrin mediates chloride reabsorption from the lumen (in exchange for bicarbonate secretion), not chloride secretion into the lumen. Downregulating pendrin would reduce both chloride reabsorption and bicarbonate secretion, not increase luminal chloride.

questions.topic: Intercalated cell function in acid-base balance

questions.stem: Based on Figure 1, the partial recovery of arterial pH from 7.22 on day 3 to 7.30 on day 7, despite continued NH4Cl administration, is best explained by:

choices.A: Resolution of respiratory compensation, with PaCO2 returning toward normal values
choices.B: Hepatic conversion of NH4Cl to urea, which neutralizes the acid load over several days
choices.C: Progressive increases in renal ammoniagenesis and titratable acid excretion that partially offset the sustained acid load
choices.D: Delayed mobilization of bone calcium carbonate, which provides extracellular buffer over days

explanations.A: Resolution of respiratory compensation would mean PaCO2 rises back toward 40 mmHg. Because the low PaCO2 was helping to offset the acidosis, its resolution would cause pH to fall, not rise. The improvement in pH between days 3 and 7 reflects additional renal compensation, not loss of respiratory compensation.
explanations.B: Hepatic conversion of ammonium to urea is not a neutralizing process. In fact, the metabolism of NH4Cl generates H+ when NH4+ is incorporated into urea because the bicarbonate consumed during ureagenesis is not regenerated. This hepatic pathway is part of the mechanism by which NH4Cl produces acidosis, not a corrective response.
explanations.C: The passage documents that urinary ammonium excretion increased 4.4-fold and titratable acid excretion doubled by day 7, coinciding with upregulation of glutaminase, GDH, PEPCK, NHE3, and H+-ATPase. These progressive renal adaptations enhance the kidney's capacity to excrete the ongoing acid load. Because the NH4Cl challenge continues, pH does not fully normalize, but the increasing renal acid excretion accounts for the partial recovery observed between days 3 and 7.
explanations.D: While bone mineral can serve as a buffer reservoir in very prolonged metabolic acidosis, the 7-day timeframe and the robust renal adaptations documented in the passage make renal compensation the dominant mechanism. Bone buffering is a slower, chronic adaptation and would not account for the specific changes in urinary ammonium and titratable acid excretion described.

questions.passage:
A 3-day-old male neonate was admitted to the neonatal intensive care unit with progressive lethargy, poor feeding, and tachypnea. The infant was born at term following an uncomplicated pregnancy and appeared well at delivery, but clinical deterioration began within the first 48 hours. Initial laboratory evaluation showed a plasma ammonia concentration of 980 μmol/L (reference: 10–35 μmol/L), arterial pH 7.51, PaCO2 22 mmHg, and plasma [HCO3−] 18 mEq/L. Plasma amino acid analysis revealed markedly elevated glutamine at 1,420 μmol/L (reference: 400–700 μmol/L) and low citrulline at 3 μmol/L (reference: 15–40 μmol/L). Urinary orotic acid was significantly elevated at 145 mmol/mol creatinine (reference: <5 mmol/mol creatinine).

The infant was started on intravenous sodium benzoate and sodium phenylacetate, along with intravenous arginine supplementation. Hemodialysis was initiated to rapidly lower the plasma ammonia concentration. Dietary protein was restricted, and essential amino acids were provided with caloric support from glucose and lipid infusions.

Genetic testing identified a hemizygous pathogenic variant in the OTC gene located on the X chromosome, confirming a diagnosis of ornithine transcarbamylase (OTC) deficiency. Family history revealed that a maternal uncle had died in infancy from an undiagnosed neurological condition. The infant's mother was asymptomatic but was confirmed to carry the same pathogenic variant upon subsequent testing.
questions.stem: The elevated urinary orotic acid concentration in this patient is best explained by accumulation of which compound proximal to the enzymatic block?

choices.A: Ornithine, which is diverted into pyrimidine synthesis when it cannot be used by OTC
choices.B: Carbamoyl phosphate, which exits the mitochondria and enters the cytosolic pyrimidine synthesis pathway
choices.C: Argininosuccinate, which is degraded to orotic acid when it accumulates beyond normal levels
choices.D: N-acetylglutamate, which inhibits downstream pyrimidine degradation and causes orotic acid retention

explanations.A: Ornithine is a substrate for OTC and does not accumulate proximal to the block; in OTC deficiency, ornithine levels are typically normal or slightly low because the cycle is not consuming it efficiently. Ornithine is not a precursor for pyrimidine synthesis and cannot be converted to orotic acid.
explanations.B: In OTC deficiency, carbamoyl phosphate synthetase I continues to produce carbamoyl phosphate in the mitochondrial matrix, but OTC cannot condense it with ornithine to form citrulline. The accumulating carbamoyl phosphate exits the mitochondria and enters the cytosol, where it is used by the pyrimidine biosynthesis pathway. Aspartate transcarbamylase condenses carbamoyl phosphate with aspartate to form carbamoyl aspartate, which is converted through dihydroorotate to orotate (orotic acid). This overflow pathway explains the characteristic orotic aciduria seen in OTC deficiency.
explanations.C: Argininosuccinate is produced downstream of the OTC step by argininosuccinate synthetase. Because citrulline production is severely impaired in OTC deficiency, argininosuccinate cannot be formed in significant quantities. Elevated argininosuccinate is a hallmark of argininosuccinate lyase deficiency, not OTC deficiency.
explanations.D: N-acetylglutamate is an obligate allosteric activator of carbamoyl phosphate synthetase I. It is not a urea cycle intermediate and its accumulation does not feed into pyrimidine synthesis or cause orotic acid retention. NAG synthase activity and NAG levels are regulated independently of the OTC reaction.

questions.topic: Urea cycle defects and orotic aciduria

questions.stem: The arterial blood gas results showing pH 7.51, PaCO2 22 mmHg, and [HCO3−] 18 mEq/L are most consistent with:

choices.A: Metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
choices.D: Primary respiratory alkalosis, likely driven by hyperammonemia-induced central hyperventilation

explanations.A: Metabolic acidosis produces a pH below 7.35 as its defining feature. This patient's pH is 7.51, which is alkalemic. Although the bicarbonate is mildly low at 18 mEq/L, this reflects renal compensation for the primary respiratory alkalosis, not a primary metabolic acid-base disorder.
explanations.B: A combined metabolic and respiratory acidosis would drive pH well below 7.35, as both processes generate acidemia. The patient's elevated pH of 7.51 is incompatible with any form of acidosis as the primary disturbance.
explanations.C: Metabolic alkalosis would present with elevated plasma bicarbonate as the primary derangement. This patient's bicarbonate is low at 18 mEq/L, ruling out metabolic alkalosis. The low PaCO2 is the primary disturbance, not a compensatory response to elevated bicarbonate.
explanations.D: The elevated pH (7.51) combined with a markedly low PaCO2 (22 mmHg) indicates a primary respiratory alkalosis. Severe hyperammonemia directly stimulates central respiratory centers, producing hyperventilation and a fall in PaCO2. The mildly reduced bicarbonate represents renal compensation for the sustained respiratory alkalosis. This pattern distinguishes neonatal hyperammonemia from organic acidemias, which typically present with primary metabolic acidosis.

questions.stem: Sodium benzoate was administered to this patient because it:

choices.A: conjugates with glycine to form hippurate, providing an alternative nitrogen excretion pathway that bypasses the urea cycle
choices.B: directly inhibits carbamoyl phosphate synthetase I to reduce further ammonia incorporation into carbamoyl phosphate
choices.C: provides ornithine to the urea cycle by serving as an ornithine precursor after hepatic metabolism
choices.D: chelates free ammonium ions in plasma, forming a stable non-toxic complex cleared by the kidneys

explanations.A: Sodium benzoate is conjugated with glycine in the liver by the enzyme glycine N-acyltransferase to form hippuric acid (hippurate), which is efficiently excreted by the kidneys. Each mole of hippurate excreted removes one mole of nitrogen from the body. As glycine is consumed, the body must resynthesize it using nitrogen from the free ammonia pool, effectively diverting waste nitrogen away from the defective urea cycle and into a functional renal excretion pathway.
explanations.B: Sodium benzoate does not inhibit CPS I. Inhibiting CPS I would reduce carbamoyl phosphate production but would also eliminate any residual urea cycle activity. The therapeutic strategy is to provide alternative nitrogen disposal, not to shut down ammonia-handling enzymes.
explanations.C: Arginine, not sodium benzoate, is administered to provide ornithine to the urea cycle. Arginine is cleaved by arginase to yield ornithine and urea. Benzoate has no structural or metabolic relationship to ornithine biosynthesis.
explanations.D: Sodium benzoate does not chelate ammonium ions. Its mechanism involves covalent conjugation with the amino acid glycine, not direct binding to ammonia or ammonium. There is no recognized ammonia-chelation mechanism for benzoate.

questions.stem: The mother's asymptomatic carrier status despite harboring the pathogenic OTC variant is best explained by:

choices.A: Autosomal recessive inheritance, which requires homozygosity for clinical expression
choices.B: Random X-inactivation, which allows hepatocytes expressing the normal allele to provide sufficient OTC activity
choices.C: Mitochondrial inheritance with low heteroplasmy in the mother's hepatocytes
choices.D: Genomic imprinting that silences the maternally inherited OTC allele in female carriers

explanations.A: OTC deficiency follows X-linked inheritance, not autosomal recessive. The OTC gene is located on the X chromosome, as stated in the passage. The mother has only one copy of the pathogenic variant and is a heterozygous carrier, which is inconsistent with an autosomal recessive model.
explanations.B: Because the OTC gene is X-linked, each of the mother's cells randomly inactivates one X chromosome during early embryonic development. In hepatocytes that inactivate the X carrying the pathogenic variant and express the normal allele, functional OTC enzyme is produced. If a sufficient proportion of hepatocytes express the normal allele, the mother maintains adequate urea cycle activity under normal dietary protein loads and remains clinically asymptomatic. This X-inactivation mosaicism also explains the variable expressivity observed among female OTC carriers.
explanations.C: The OTC gene is a nuclear gene located on the X chromosome, not a mitochondrial gene. Although the OTC protein functions within the mitochondrial matrix, the gene is transcribed from nuclear DNA and follows Mendelian X-linked inheritance, not maternal mitochondrial inheritance.
explanations.D: The OTC gene is not subject to genomic imprinting, and imprinting does not selectively silence maternally inherited alleles on the X chromosome. X-inactivation is random with respect to parental origin in most tissues, and the mother's asymptomatic status reflects mosaic expression due to lyonization, not imprinting.

questions.stem: Arginine supplementation in this patient primarily serves to:

choices.A: Activate nitric oxide synthase in cerebral vasculature to reduce ammonia-induced brain edema
choices.B: Inhibit renal glutaminase, thereby reducing peripheral ammonia production
choices.C: Stimulate N-acetylglutamate synthase, increasing CPS I activity and carbamoyl phosphate production
choices.D: Provide ornithine via arginase cleavage, maintaining residual urea cycle flux and supplying an amino acid that becomes conditionally essential

explanations.A: While arginine is a substrate for nitric oxide synthase, vasodilatory NO production is not the therapeutic rationale for arginine supplementation in urea cycle defects. The primary goal is to replenish ornithine to sustain whatever residual cycle activity remains and to supply arginine as a conditionally essential amino acid.
explanations.B: Arginine does not inhibit glutaminase. Renal glutaminase plays a role in acid-base homeostasis and nitrogen excretion, and inhibiting it would impair renal ammonia production, which would be unhelpful in the acute management of hyperammonemia where nitrogen needs to be excreted.
explanations.C: Arginine is a positive effector of N-acetylglutamate synthase, but increasing CPS I activity would generate more carbamoyl phosphate. In OTC deficiency, carbamoyl phosphate already accumulates because OTC cannot use it. Further stimulating its production would worsen the overflow into pyrimidine synthesis and increase orotic acid excretion, which is counterproductive.
explanations.D: In OTC deficiency, citrulline production is severely impaired, which reduces formation of all downstream urea cycle intermediates including arginine itself. Exogenous arginine is cleaved by arginase in the liver to regenerate ornithine and release one molecule of urea. The ornithine can then re-enter the mitochondria and sustain whatever residual OTC activity may be present. Additionally, because endogenous arginine synthesis is compromised, arginine becomes a conditionally essential amino acid that must be supplied exogenously.
